entries.description:
Find specific passages inside long AV transcripts via per-chunk embedding similarity. Use this when you want to quote what was said, not just "which video". Returns the parent video metadata plus the matched chunk text and its start offset.

When to use: Find WHERE in videos/podcasts/spaces a topic is discussed — returns AI summaries of the matching passages plus an opaque ordering offset (NOT a playback timestamp). Use when you need "the part of the talk where they discuss X".

Returns: Array of AV items with AI summary, created_at (the recording's date — use it to judge and cite how recent the talk is), start_offset (an opaque transcript chunk offset for ordering passages within a recording — NOT playback seconds; do not render it as a timestamp or build a deep-link from it), and link. Transcript text itself is never returned — cite the link + the passage summary.

query.description: Topic or phrase to match against transcript passages

results.description: Array of AV items: AI summary, start_offset (opaque transcript chunk offset, not seconds), link.

text.description: Present instead of the data fields when the tool reports a plain-text message.

entries.description:
Find articles, AV content, events, and tweets that are semantically related to a project. Uses embedding similarity to find cross-domain content that DISCUSSES the project. Supports optional date/source/type filters for scoped research.

When to use: All published content related to ONE project, keyed BY SLUG (resolve a name to a slug with search_directory first). For an open-ended topic use search_content_semantic; for entity-name mentions (incl. tokens/orgs) where you only have a name use find_content_by_entity.

Returns: Content grouped by type with AI summaries + source links + date, filtered to the project, recency-aware so recent coverage (incidents, launches) surfaces alongside canonical content. response_format 'concise' (external default) omits long_summary; pass 'detailed' to include it.

date_field.description: For articles only: force a specific date field. Default is COALESCE(publishing_date, created_at).

date_start.description: ISO date/timestamp lower bound (inclusive). For articles, applies to COALESCE(publishing_date, created_at) by default to avoid stale re-scrape pollution.

limit.description: Max results per content type (default 20). Effective rows = limit × number of types — keep it small and narrow `types` instead of raising it.

response_format.description: 'concise' omits long_summary from each row (smaller payloads); 'detailed' keeps it. Default: detailed.

sources.description: Filter by source. For articles: domain. For AV: channel. For events: platform. For tweets: user_screen_name.

types.description: Restrict to specific content types. Default: all 9 (knowledge = internal dated notes). Narrow this for smaller, faster results — fanning out across all types multiplies the row count.

articles.description: Related articles with AI summaries + source links.

text.description: Present instead of the data fields when the tool reports a plain-text message.

entries.description:
Find articles, AV, events, governance proposals, and SCF submissions that mention a specific named entity. Uses LLM-extracted entities (project/person/organization/token) from content_entities. Case-insensitive match on entity_name or exact match on normalized_slug. Use this when you want exact entity grounding, not semantic similarity.

When to use: Find content mentioning a NAMED entity (project, organization, token) by case-insensitive name match with LLM-confidence filtering — works even when the entity has no directory slug. If you have a slug, prefer find_content_about_project.

Returns: Array of content items with the matched entity, confidence score, and content metadata.

Catalog note: entity_type "person" yields all-empty groups on this lane even for heavily covered people (live-verified 2026-07-03) — an empty person result is lane behavior, NOT evidence of absence. Use entity types project, organization, or token; for a person, use search_content_semantic with the person name instead.

content_type.description: Restrict to a specific content type

entity.description: Entity name (case-insensitive) or normalized slug. Examples: "Aquarius", "aquarius", "StellarOrg".

entity_type.description: Restrict to a specific entity type

min_confidence.description: Minimum LLM confidence to include (0.0-1.0). Default 0.5 filters out weak matches.

results.description: Array of content items with matched entity, confidence, metadata.

text.description: Present instead of the data fields when the tool reports a plain-text message.

entries.description:
Find projects semantically similar to a given project using embedding cosine similarity. More accurate than tag/category matching.

When to use: Find projects similar to a known project (by slug) via embedding similarity — better than tag/category matching. For keyword search use search_directory.

Returns: Array of projects ranked by cosine similarity: slug, title, description, similarity score.

results.description: Array of similar projects: slug, title, description, similarity score.

text.description: Present instead of the data fields when the tool reports a plain-text message.

entries.description:
Search SCF (Stellar Community Fund) submissions by embedding similarity. Pass a natural-language query for topic/category discovery or an existing submission slug to find similar prior proposals. Returns title, description, category, round, award_type, the linked_project_slug (the primary directory project, if any), and linked_project_slugs (the full set of directory projects this submission credits).

When to use: Explore SCF submissions by topic (natural-language query) or find proposals similar to an existing one (slug). Query and slug are mutually exclusive.

Returns: Array of submissions ranked by similarity, with round/category/funding metadata, linked_project_slug (the primary credited directory project) and linked_project_slugs (the full set of credited directory projects).

category.description: Restrict to a specific category (e.g. "Developer Tooling")

query.description: Natural-language query (e.g. "ZK proofs for compliance"). Mutually exclusive with `slug`.

round.description: Restrict to a specific SCF round (e.g. "SCF #29")

slug.description: Existing SCF submission slug (e.g. "recsAQHCghw9HIUvF") — returns semantically similar submissions. Mutually exclusive with `query`.

results.description: Array of similar submissions with round/category/funding, linked_project_slug (primary credited project) and linked_project_slugs (full credited set).

text.description: Present instead of the data fields when the tool reports a plain-text message.

entries.description:
Get the controlled vocabulary of categories. Use these exact values when setting project categories.

When to use: Get the exact controlled vocabulary of project categories before filtering or labeling by category. Do not invent category strings.

Returns: { count, categories: [...] } — each category: { id, name, slug }.

entries.description:
Get full details of a single document by ID from any collection. The av collection accepts either "videos" or "av" — they map to the same table.

When to use: Fetch one document by collection + numeric id, after a list/search tool gave you the id.

Returns: One document. External callers get the summary projection (title, url, summary, long_summary, tags, dates) — full body text and transcripts are not exposed; cite the returned url instead.

collection.description: Content collection (videos and av both target the av table)

entries.description:
Get details of a project from the ecosystem directory by slug. Pass compact=true to get a slim writer-friendly shape (~500 chars: slug, title, short description, category, tags, twitter_handles, website, other_names) — recommended for writer agents that just need to verify a slug and read @ handles. Default returns the full row (~8k chars including conflict tracking, source values, field policy).

When to use: Fetch one project BY SLUG. If you only have a name, resolve it to a slug with search_directory first — a guessed slug will 404. Pass compact=true when you only need identity fields for writing/citation (cuts ~95% of the payload).

Returns: One project object: slug, title, description, category, tags, links (website, x, github, …), based_in, operating_region, SCF awards. Internal-only fields (source values, conflicts, hidden links) are never included for external callers.

compact.description: Return slim writer shape instead of full row. Cuts ~95% of payload size.

entries.description:
Get the controlled vocabulary of project tags. Use these exact tag values when setting directory project tags.

When to use: Get the controlled tag vocabulary for DIRECTORY PROJECTS. For article/AV content tags use get_tags_vocabulary instead.

Returns: { count, tags: [...] } — each tag: { id, name, slug }.

tags.description: project tag objects ({ id, name, slug })

Get all distinct region values (based_in + operating_region) currently used in the directory

When to use: Get the distinct region values (based_in + operating_region) in use, before filtering projects geographically.

entries.description:
Given a content item (article/av/event/research) return the directory projects it mentions, via the content_projects junction.

When to use: You have a CONTENT item (article/av/event/research id) and want the ecosystem projects it mentions — the "Mentioned projects" on lumenloop.com. The reverse direction (project → content) is find_content_about_project.

Returns: Array of mentioned projects with public info (slug, title, description, category).

content_id.description: Numeric id of the content row (from get_document / search results).

content_type.description: Content type. Canonical singular: article | av | event | research | proposal (plural/alias accepted).

entries.description:
Get Stellar Community Fund submissions linked to a project, including the full application/proposal text (markdown: sections, milestones, deliverables, budget detail), funding amount, award status, round, and category. Searches by slug or project name — pass at least one.

When to use: Get Stellar Community Fund submissions linked to a specific project. Pass slug (resolve a name via search_directory) or a fuzzy name. For topic-based exploration across all submissions use find_similar_scf_submissions.

Returns: { count, submissions: [...] } — each: title, round, category, award_type, funding amounts, description, the full application/proposal text (markdown), links, linked_project_slug (the primary credited directory project) and linked_project_slugs (the full set of directory projects this submission credits).

slug.description: Project slug (preferred — resolve names via search_directory first)

submissions.description: SCF submissions: title, round, category, award_type, funding, full application/proposal text, links, linked_project_slug (primary credited project), linked_project_slugs (full credited set)

entries.description:
Get the controlled vocabulary of content tags from the orchestrator. These tags are used for articles and AV content, NOT for directory projects.

When to use: Get the controlled tag vocabulary for CONTENT (articles/AV). For directory project tags use get_project_tags_vocabulary instead.

Returns: Array of content tag objects (id, name).

entries.description:
Browse/enumerate ONE collection by structured criteria — status, source, date, time period (upcoming/past events) — with sorting and pagination. Its optional "search" is the SAME exact case-insensitive SUBSTRING match on title and URL as search_documents (not body text, not meaning). For conceptual or question-phrased discovery, or when you do not know which collection holds the answer, use search_content_semantic instead. The av collection accepts either "videos" or "av".

When to use: Browse/page through a content collection (articles, events, videos/av, research) with filters and sorting. For text matching use search_documents; for meaning-based discovery use search_content_semantic.

Returns: Page of document summaries plus pagination info. External callers get summary projection: title, url, slug, summary, long_summary, tags, dates — never full article bodies or AV transcripts.

collection.description: Content collection to query (videos and av both target the av table)

period.description: Events only: filter by time period

sort.description: Sort field. Articles: processed_at, publishing_date, created_at, domain, source. Events: start_at, processed_at, created_at. Videos: processed_at, publishing_date, created_at, source. Jobs: updated_at, last_seen_at, published_at, created_at.

items.description: Document summaries: title, url, slug, summary, long_summary, tags, dates.

text.description: Present instead of the data fields when the tool reports a plain-text message.

entries.description:
List research drafts with optional filters. Used by drafter agents for dedup ("has anything similar been drafted lately?") and by agents needing to reference prior work. Returns preview fields only — call get_document(collection="research", id) for full body.

When to use: Browse LumenLoop's own published research/editorial pieces (previews) — id, title, summary, links.

Returns: Array of research previews: id, title, format, status, summary, created_at — not full bodies.

format.description: Filter by format ('sources' = partner source packs, 'structured' = partner schema-fills).

since.description: ISO date/timestamp; only rows with created_at >= this.

source.description: Filter by source prefix match (e.g. "agent:" for all agent-authored).

research.description: Research previews: id, title, format, status, summary, created_at.

entries.description:
Search all content semantically. Embeds the query text and finds similar articles, AV, events, and tweets via pgvector cosine distance. Use this (not search_documents/list_documents) whenever the query is conceptual or phrased as a question, or when you do not know which collection holds the answer; reserve search_documents for exact title/URL keyword lookup. Supports date/source/type filters — use these for scoped research queries like "find Stellar DeFi content from March" or "what did SDF post about privacy".

When to use: Meaning-based search across ALL published content types at once (articles, AV, events, research) — the default discovery tool for "what has been said about X". For exact-term lookup in one collection use search_documents; for content about one specific project use find_content_about_project. For "latest/current" ecosystem facts (protocol version, program/fund status, recent incidents or launches) prefer this over live web — results are dated and the corpus updates continuously; cite the date. Use web only for facts the corpus cannot hold, e.g. upstream package/registry pins (crates.io, npm, GitHub release tags).

Returns: Results grouped by content type, each with AI summary, source url, date, and similarity score — never full text. Cite the returned url. response_format 'concise' (external default) omits long_summary; pass 'detailed' to include it.

date_field.description: For articles only: force a specific date field. Default is COALESCE(publishing_date, created_at).

date_start.description: ISO date/timestamp lower bound (inclusive). For articles, applies to COALESCE(publishing_date, created_at) by default to avoid stale re-scrape pollution.

limit.description: Max results per content type (default 20). Effective rows = limit × number of types — keep it small and narrow `types` instead of raising it.

response_format.description: 'concise' omits long_summary from each row (smaller payloads); 'detailed' keeps it. Default: detailed.

sources.description: Filter by source. For articles: domain. For AV: channel. For events: platform. For tweets: user_screen_name.

types.description: Restrict to specific content types. Default: all 9 (knowledge = internal dated notes). Narrow this for smaller, faster results — fanning out across all types multiplies the row count.

articles.description: Article hits: AI summary, source url, date, similarity.

text.description: Present instead of the data fields when the tool reports a plain-text message.

entries.description:
Search the Stellar ecosystem directory for projects by name, description, or category (substring match). If a multi-word or conceptual query matches no substring, it automatically falls back to semantic project discovery (response carries match_mode: "semantic") so you still get the closest projects.

When to use: Start here to find a Stellar ecosystem project by name, keyword, or category when you do not know its slug. Substring match, but a multi-word/conceptual query that hits no substring auto-falls back to semantic project discovery, so you can also use it for "what RWA/wallet/oracle projects exist" style asks. For "projects like X" use find_similar_projects_semantic; for content ABOUT a project use find_content_about_project.

Returns: { count, projects: [...] } — each project: slug, title, description, category, tags. When the substring match is empty and the semantic fallback fires, the response also carries match_mode: "semantic" + a note, and rows include a similarity score. Take the slug into get_project for full details.

Catalog note: prefer this lane plus find_content_about_project when a what is X or who builds X question wants narrative editorial context about a named ecosystem project; the scout project search returns structured fields only.

query.description: Search query (substring match on title, description, other_names, and category)

projects.description: project matches: slug, title, description, category, tags, website

text.description: Present instead of the data fields when the tool reports a plain-text message.

entries.description:
Exact keyword lookup: case-insensitive SUBSTRING match on title and URL only (NOT body text, NOT meaning) within ONE collection. Use when you already know wording that appears in a title or URL (e.g. find the article about a known announcement). For conceptual or question-phrased queries use search_content_semantic; to browse a collection by status/date/source use list_documents. The av collection accepts either "videos" or "av".

When to use: Exact/keyword match on title or URL within one collection (e.g. find the article about a known announcement). For conceptual queries ("content about cross-border payments") use search_content_semantic.

Returns: Array of matching document summaries (same external projection as list_documents).

collection.description: Content collection to search (videos and av both target the av table)

items.description: Matching document summaries (same projection as list_documents).

text.description: Present instead of the data fields when the tool reports a plain-text message.

entries.description: Cross-event Stellar ecosystem analytics rollup. Returns the cross-everything ecosystem rollup — the macro totals that single-event or single-cluster tools can't answer alone. dimension=all (default) or slice to hackathons (totalEvents, upcoming/active/completed counts, totalPrizePoolUSD, totalRegisteredHackers — live from DoraHacks), categories (project-count distribution + scfFunded + hackathon winners per category), or funding (scfAwardedProjects, scfTotalDistributedUSD, meanAwardUSD, per-round breakdown, and the post-hackathon Built/In-Progress/Abandoned status funnel). Use when: 'what's the overall state of Stellar hackathons/grants', 'total SCF funding distributed / mean award size', 'how much prize money across all hackathons', 'how many projects get built vs abandoned after hackathons'. Not for: crowdedness or whitespace per category → use getClusters; a ranked list of top/active projects → use getLeaderboard; one specific hackathon's details → use getHackathon; comparing two hackathons head-to-head → use compareHackathons. Use for ecosystem-wide totals and macro stats — project counts by category, total SCF funding, hackathon totals, and developer-activity aggregates across the whole ecosystem. Includes ecosystem-wide DeFi TVL rollups (as-of dated — never an exact to-the-dollar live figure) and totals across all events/categories.

Compare 2–5 hackathons side-by-side. Side-by-side comparison of 2–5 named hackathons (by slug). Returns each event's snapshot (dates, status, source, prizePoolUSD, hackersCount, submissionCount, winnerCount, prizePerWinnerUSD where derivable) plus a deltas block flagging the spreads agents care about — prize-pool range, submission-count range, prize-per-winner, registered-hacker counts — with human-readable notes like '2× spread'. Unresolved slugs come back as source:"not-found" and are listed in deltas.notes without inflating returned. Use when: 'which Stellar hackathon should I enter', 'how did [event A] compare to [event B] on prizes/turnout', 'was [A] or [B] bigger'. Requires ≥2 known slugs (max 5; iterate beyond that) — resolve them via getHackathons first. Not for: one event's deep detail/winners → use getHackathon; discovering/listing events → use getHackathons; ecosystem-wide totals across ALL events → use analyzeEcosystem.

Deep code answer about a Stellar repo (routing × DeepWiki). Get a source-grounded ANSWER to a deep code question about a Stellar repo — not just a link. StellarLight routes the question to the authoritative repo (error/result codes, consensus/SCP, XDR → stellar/stellar-core; Horizon → stellar/go; RPC → stellar/stellar-rpc; SEP reference impls; SDKs), then DeepWiki answers from that repo's internals with source files. Use when: 'where is X defined / how does Y work' for a Stellar internal. Not for: which repos/projects exist → use scout.searchRepos or scout.searchProjects; ecosystem docs / SEP text / audits → use scout.searchResearch. Degrades gracefully: if DeepWiki is unavailable you still get the routed authoritative repo + its deepWikiUrl. Also for 'how do I / where is / show me the implementation of' deep code questions answered from the authoritative repo's internals.

Catalog note: the upstream payload carries its own ok flag at data.ok — distinct from the envelope call-status ok.

q.description: The deep code question (e.g. 'where are transaction result codes defined').

repo.description: Optional owner/name to pin the repo (e.g. stellar/stellar-core). Omit to auto-route.

alternateRepos.description: Other authoritative repos for this concept. Always present ([] when none).

answer.description: DeepWiki source-grounded answer; null if DeepWiki had no answer (routed repo still returned).

answered.description: Always present, including when routedVia is null (then false).

codeVerified.description: Code-verified truth from analyzing the routed repo's ACTUAL source — qualify the answer with it: a deployable contract on a supported soroban-sdk is authoritative; tooling that merely uses Stellar is not. Null until code-scanned.

note.description: Present when routing failed entirely — a hint to use search_repos or pin ?repo=.

note2.description: Present when a repo routed but DeepWiki had no answer.

repoMeta.description: Freshness/status of the routed repo from the StellarLight index — attach lastCommitAt as the as-of date when citing the answer. Null when the repo isn't indexed or nothing routed.

routedVia.description: How the repo was chosen: explicit | canonical | search. null when nothing routed.

sources.description: Always present; fields are null when nothing was routed.

entries.description: Search Stellar builders. The Stellar people directory — curated builder PROFILES (synced from Stellar Passport): displayName, githubUsername, bio, roleTitle, location, scfTier, and the projects[] each has shipped. Free-text q/skill searches across bio + role + project names/descriptions; location filters by place; featured builders sort first. Use when: 'find me a teammate/collaborator who has shipped X', 'Stellar devs in Lagos who've done Soroban', 'who can I hire for an anchor build' — i.e. you want a PERSON to contact. Not for: a funded project/product or 'who built X (the company)' → use searchProjects; the GitHub repo/code itself → use searchRepos; ecosystem-wide dev counts/activity stats → use getLeaderboard. Use for recruiting or hiring: find developers, engineers, contributors, or teammates to recruit or hire by skill, SCF tier, or track record of SCF awards. Filter/find PEOPLE by region (Latin America, India, Africa, Asia, Europe) or by stack — e.g. 'experienced Soroban devs I could reach out to', 'Rust builders to hire', or 'builders in LatAm working on payments'.

limit.description: Max results per page. The default and cap VARY by endpoint (e.g. projects/search 20/100, builders 50/200, leaderboard 50/300, research 8/25). A value below 1 or above the cap is clamped, not rejected.

offset.description: Number of matching rows to skip before returning (pagination). Page until offset + meta.counts.returned >= meta.counts.total.

q.description: Free-text filter over bio / role / projects (accepts `skill`/`tech` as aliases)

skill.description: Filter by skill/tech mentioned in bio

entries.description:
Recent changes to the API, MCP tools, and client. A curated, latest-first feed of contract-affecting changes — new/removed endpoints & tools, param/enum changes, description rewrites. Point an agent here to see what changed lately without reading git history. Use when: you cached the API surface earlier and want to know what moved before relying on it, or you're debugging a field/param that changed. Not for: the actual ecosystem data → use the relevant search endpoint; current health / source freshness / version → use scout.getStatus.

Catalog note: the upstream payload carries its own ok flag at data.ok — distinct from the envelope call-status ok.

since.description: Only entries on/after this ISO date (YYYY-MM-DD).

entries.description: Topic clusters with crowdedness scores. Groups the active Stellar project directory into topic clusters and scores each on crowdedness (1–10, log-scaled) so you can see saturated vs underbuilt lanes — i.e. where to build. Each cluster returns size, crowdedness, scfFundedCount, scfTotalUSD, hackathonWinnerCount, and up to 5 sample projects; cluster by dimension=category (coarse 7-cat) or dimension=types (finer: Wallet/DEX/Lending/RWA/Oracle…), or pass key/type to get one cluster. Use when: 'what's the most crowded category on Stellar', 'show me an underbuilt/whitespace area', 'how many projects/funded projects are in RWA vs wallets', 'where is the competition thin'. Not for: ranking the top projects/repos by stars or activity → use getLeaderboard; ecosystem-wide totals (events + funding + status funnel) → use analyzeEcosystem; finding/looking up an actual named project in a category → use searchProjects. Use for 'where should I build', 'what's underbuilt or saturated', 'which categories are crowded' opportunity-gap questions across the project directory. The market-map view: where the ecosystem is crowded versus whitespace, how many projects exist per category (wallets, DeFi, NFT, anchors), and where there is room to build.

minSize.description: Only include clusters with at least N projects

entries.description:
Get one hackathon's full detail. Full detail for ONE hackathon by slug — its metadata plus every submission with placement, prize amount, prize track, post-hack status (Built/In Progress/Abandoned), and scfAwarded flag; derives winners, per-tracks aggregates (prize $ + winner/submission counts), and a stats outcome funnel. Dual-shape: curated events return full DB detail; DoraHacks-only events read submissions/winners/tracks live from DoraHacks (degrading to a curated winner roster + meta.note when the live feed is unavailable). Use when: 'who won [event] / its soroban track', 'what projects were submitted to [event]', 'what tracks did [event] have and what did they pay', 'how many [event] submissions are still being built'. Needs an exact slug — get it from getHackathons first if unknown. Not for: listing/browsing many events → use getHackathons; comparing stats across 2+ events → use compareHackathons; ecosystem-wide aggregates → use analyzeEcosystem; a winning project's own repo/funding/status outside the hackathon context → use searchProjects / searchRepos.

Catalog note: winner order is only meaningful when hackathonPlacement is ordinal (1st Place, 2nd Place, ...) and placementRank is a number; many events label every winner just Winners with placementRank null — there the winners array order is NOT a ranking, so never assert finishing order from list position (live-verified 2026-07-03).

meta.description: Standard meta block included on every list response

returned.description: Rows in this page (post limit/offset slice)

total.description: Rows matching the filter before slicing (paginated endpoints). Page until offset + returned >= total.

winners.description: Winner entries. Ordering contract: placementRank is the ONLY per-entry ordering signal — never infer finishing order from array position; check winnersRanked first.

winnersRanked.description: Whether the winners array order is a ranking. true = ordinal placements (sorted by placementRank, winners[0] is 1st place); false = tier-labeled winners (all placementRank null — array order is meaningless, treat as an unordered set); null = no winners recorded.

entries.description: List Stellar hackathons. Browse/LIST Stellar hackathon events — a merged, de-duplicated feed of curated Payload events (rich detail, internal pages) + live DoraHacks events (SDF org IDs 3096/3853), sorted upcoming→active→completed then newest-first. Each row carries name, slug, dates, status, organizer, source, and (for DoraHacks) prizePoolUSD + hackersCount. Filter by status (upcoming|active|completed), organizer slug, or source (curated|dorahacks). Use when: 'what Stellar hackathons are coming up / running now / recently ended', 'list SDF hackathons', or you need the slug of an event before drilling in. When a forward-looking query (status=upcoming|active) returns zero, it adds meta.fallbackChannels (BuildOnStellar, stellarlight.xyz, DoraHacks) — surface those rather than dead-ending. Not for: one event's winners/submissions/tracks → use getHackathon; comparing 2+ named events → use compareHackathons; ecosystem-wide rollups (total prize pools, category/funding totals across ALL events) → use analyzeEcosystem; RFPs/bounties/grants → use getRfps.

limit.description: Max results per page. The default and cap VARY by endpoint (e.g. projects/search 20/100, builders 50/200, leaderboard 50/300, research 8/25). A value below 1 or above the cap is clamped, not rejected.

source.description: Restrict to one feed (curated vs DoraHacks)

allOf.description: Standard meta block included on every list response

returned.description: Rows in this page (post limit/offset slice)

total.description: Rows matching the filter before slicing (paginated endpoints). Page until offset + returned >= total.

fallbackChannels.description: Present when the query returns 0 events — a summary plus live announcement channels to point the user at. NOTE: an OBJECT, not an array.

entries.description: Stellar ecosystem developer activity. Returns a ranked list of active Stellar projects (default top 50, max 300) sortable by sort=activity|stars|issues over a range (7d/30d/90d/1y/all), with per-project GitHub rollups (totalStars, openIssuesTotal, lastActivityAt, repoCount) and scfAwarded; optional category filter and format=csv. Also bundles an Electric Capital ecosystem dev macro block (28-day active / Stellar-only / multichain dev counts, commits28d, full-time/part-time/one-time dev splits). Use when: 'who/what are the top/most-active Stellar projects', 'most-starred projects', 'which projects shipped recently (last 30d)', 'how many active Stellar devs / how does Stellar's dev activity look', or you need a CSV/Dune-style export of ranked projects. Not for: category counts or crowded-vs-underbuilt whitespace → use getClusters; ecosystem-wide hackathon/funding/status-funnel totals → use analyzeEcosystem; finding a specific project's profile/funding/competitors → use searchProjects; ranking individual developers (this ranks PROJECTS, plus an EC macro snapshot — it does not list named devs) → use getBuilders.

category.description: Filter the leaderboard to one project category (e.g. 'Tooling', 'Infrastructure'). An unrecognized value returns 400 with the valid list.

limit.description: Max results per page. The default and cap VARY by endpoint (e.g. projects/search 20/100, builders 50/200, leaderboard 50/300, research 8/25). A value below 1 or above the cap is clamped, not rejected.

range.description: Activity time-window for the leaderboard. An unrecognized value returns 400.

sort.description: Order the project leaderboard. An unrecognized value returns 400 with the valid list.

entries.description: Get one partner's full profile. Full published profile for one partner by slug, including verified signals + freshness. 404 for unknown or unpublished slugs. Use when: you already know a partner's slug (from scout.getPartners) and need their full public profile — services, sectors, regions, docs, contact, freshness. Not for: discovering partners for a need → use scout.getPartners with type/sector/region/q; a project/product rather than a service provider → use scout.searchProjects.

List ecosystem partners. Published ecosystem partners (anchors, on/off ramps, infrastructure, tooling, protocols, wallets, audit firms). Each carries partner-claimed facts AND system-verified signals (GitHub activity, on-chain footprint, SCF involvement) plus a freshness object — consumers should down-rank or skip partners flagged freshness.excludeFromMatching. Fresh partners sort first. Filter by type / sector / region / accepting / q. Use when: 'who should audit my Soroban contract' (type=audit-firm), 'find an anchor or on/off-ramp in {region}', or partner discovery for an integration. Not for: projects/products that were BUILT → use searchProjects; the people who build them → use getBuilders; SCF grant briefs → use getRfps.

Catalog note: this is the partner and service-provider DIRECTORY for anchors, asset issuers, on/off ramps, stablecoin and RWA providers, audit firms, infrastructure, tooling, protocols, wallets, and integration providers; filter by region, type, sector, accepting, or q. Use for regional partner listings including common region names and aliases such as Latin America/LatAm, MENA, Africa, Europe, and Asia-Pacific. Use searchProjects for built products, apps, protocols, and projects rather than service-provider listings.

accepting.description: Set to 1 to return only partners currently accepting new clients

all.description: Set to 1 to bypass the directory quality bar. By default results include only complete, non-archived profiles (tagline + a contact path), pilot partners first; all=1 returns every published partner including placeholder-thin profiles.

limit.description: Max results per page. The default and cap VARY by endpoint (e.g. projects/search 20/100, builders 50/200, leaderboard 50/300, research 8/25). A value below 1 or above the cap is clamped, not rejected.

ramps.description: Filter by fiat-ramp capability: `on-ramp` (fiat → Stellar), `off-ramp` (Stellar → fiat), or `on-ramp,off-ramp` to require both. Unknown values return 400 with `validRamps`. Combine with `region`/`q` for corridor lookups (e.g. ramps=on-ramp&q=mexico).

region.description: Filter by region served (global, latam, africa, …)

sector.description: Filter by sector served (defi, payments, rwa, stablecoins, …)

entries.description: List Stellar RFPs (SCF-funded sponsor briefs). The curated set of Stellar RFPs / sponsor briefs (mirrors the /ideas page) — open ones are eligible for SCF grant funding when winners are picked; closed ones are past rounds kept for context. Each brief has title, description, technicalRequirements, category, quarter, and a quarter-derived status (open|closed). Filter by q, category, quarter, or status; response carries open/closed counts + the activeQuarter. Use when: 'what RFPs/bounties match my idea', 'what's the SCF funding asking for this quarter', 'is there a sponsor brief for X I could get funded to build'. Not for: projects already BUILT/funded → use searchProjects; hackathons + their prizes → use getHackathons; how-to / SCF Handbook / standards knowledge → use searchResearch. Use for 'what grants or sponsor briefs or bounties are open', 'what does the ecosystem want built', 'where can I get an SCF grant' funding-opportunity questions. Also the place to check which SCF round is currently open for submissions and its deadline, whether a past RFP (e.g. the Kelp / Hummingbot one) is closed or can still be applied to, and what the post-submission review lifecycle looks like.

category.description: Filter by RFP category (e.g. 'defi', 'payments', 'infrastructure'). An unrecognized value returns 400 with the valid category list.

status.description: Open RFPs are fundable for the current SCF quarter; closed are prior rounds

scfRound.description: SCF round identity + submission window (curated — SCF publishes no machine-readable round feed): fields are null when unconfirmed rather than guessed. Always cite asOf alongside answers built on this.

currentRound.description: Round currently open for submissions; null when no round is confirmed open as of asOf.

entries.description:
Get one skill's full content. Full detail for ONE skill by slug or display name — returns its metadata plus, for SDF official skills, the complete raw SKILL.md text under .skill.content (fetched live from skills.stellar.org); curated/community entries return metadata (and inlined content for Stellarlight's own skills). Accepts either the slug ('agentic-payments') or display name ('Agentic Payments'); 404s with a hint to list scout.listSkills if unknown. Use when: you already know a skill name and need its actual instructions / install command / SKILL.md body to follow or quote. Not for: browsing or discovering which skills exist → use listSkills; conceptual docs or standards behind a topic (not a packaged skill) → use searchResearch.

Catalog note: returns the full upstream markdown of one directory entry, suited to install/metadata questions. For reading build/integration playbooks, prefer the bundled skills. catalog entries via codemode.skill.read (sectioned, curated, pinned).

Service health + endpoint enumeration. Self-describe / health endpoint for Stellar Scout — returns service ok + version, generatedAt, per-source freshness & size (sources[]: projects, hackathons, builders, repos, ecosystemStats, sdfSkills, each with count + lastUpdatedAt + notes), recent usage stats, and an enumeration of every available /api/ endpoint. Cheap (count-only queries, no params). Use when: you need to know how fresh/large the data is before answering ('as of when?', 'how many projects are indexed?'), discover what endpoints exist, or sanity-check the API is up. Not for: the actual project/repo/research DATA itself → call the matching search tool (searchProjects, searchRepos, searchResearch); ecosystem developer-activity macro stats → use getLeaderboard.

Catalog note: the upstream payload carries its own ok health/status flag at data.ok — distinct from the envelope call-status ok.

apiVersion.description: API contract (OpenAPI) version — equals /api/openapi.json info.version. Reason about the live contract from this rather than the service version.

entries.description:
List AI skills for Stellar builders. Catalog of installable Stellar AI skills/tools — a unified, filterable list merging SDF's 7 official skills.stellar.org skills, Stellarlight + lumenloop + trusted third-party curated entries, and approved community submissions. Each entry carries an install command, kind (skill-md | mcp-server | sdk | cli | agent-kit | tool), source, repo/homepage/docs, and meta.counts.bySource; filter by source or kind. Use when: 'what Stellar AI skills / MCP servers / SDKs can I install', 'is there a skill for soroban / anchors / payments', or matching a builder's idea to the right installable tool. Not for: the full SKILL.md text or install details of ONE named skill → use getSkill; built/funded products in the ecosystem (not installable agent skills) → use searchProjects; GitHub source repos → use searchRepos; docs/standards/how-to knowledge → use searchResearch.

Catalog note: this is the live ecosystem DIRECTORY — skills plus MCP servers, SDKs, and CLIs — for discovering what exists and fetching install metadata; most entries are not mirrored skills.

AI-rank partners against a plain-language need. Describe a builder need in plain language (e.g. 'I need a USDC off-ramp in Mexico with SEP-24') and get the published partners RANKED by fit, each with a one-line reason. Grounded: only real published partners are ranked — nothing is invented. Requires the service to have AI configured; returns 503 unavailable:true otherwise (fall back to scout.getPartners filters). Use when: an agent needs a scored shortlist for a concrete integration need. Not for: browsing the full directory → scout.getPartners.

need.description: The builder's need, in plain language

entries.description: AI onboarding helpers: interview chat + profile extraction. Two modes for the get-listed flow. {mode:'chat', messages} → interview-style reply for a company describing itself. {mode:'extract', messages} → structures the transcript into partner-profile fields (tagline, services, sectors, regions, contact…; null where nothing was stated — nothing invented). 503 unavailable:true without AI configured. Use when: turning a get-listed conversation into structured profile fields. Not for: finding partners → scout.matchPartners.

Search Stellar projects (prior art / competitor lookup). Search the curated Stellar project/product directory — what's been built, by whom, with SCF-funding and live/inactive status (wallets, DEXes, anchors, lending, oracles, RWAs, tooling). Keyword + synonym match (dex→amm/swap, rosca→susu/chama) ranked by curated prominence, SDF/community verification, SCF funding, and Live status; falls back to semantic vector search when keyword hits are thin. Each result carries status, scfAwarded/scfTotalAwardedUSD, builtBy (the org/entity behind it — 'who built X'), the project's own links, a confidence score, and its top indexed repos inline. Use when: 'who/what already exists for X', 'has anyone built X', 'is there a live/funded project for X', or you need a project's funding/status/competitors. Not for: raw GitHub source repos ranked by code quality → use searchRepos; docs, SEPs, audits, how-to/feasibility knowledge → use searchResearch; category counts or whitespace → use getClusters. Use for 'who has built / is there a / which projects / give me a directory of' questions about Stellar apps, products, protocols, dApps, teams, companies and startups by category — DeFi, lending, credit, yield, AMM, DEX, swap, NFT marketplace, wallets, anchors and on/off-ramps, RWA and real-world-asset tokenization, stablecoins, payments, oracles, identity, gaming, infrastructure, tooling. Also 'is there a mature X I could integrate', 'who is building Y', projects operating as anchors, and named products (Etherfuse Stablebonds, Soroswap) — for DIRECTORY FACTS about those products only (status, funding, builder, links); for editorial/analysis content about them (articles, interviews, metrics commentary, deep dives) use content platforms instead. Also answers list/enumerate/compare questions from the curated directory: which wallets exist and how they differ, anchors and anchor infrastructure, the main DeFi projects (directory / market map), NFT marketplaces, perpetuals/derivatives DEXes, block explorers (stellar.expert, StellarChain), AMM/liquidity protocols and their yields, and specific products like Etherfuse Stablebonds (USTRY/CETES) — again as directory records, not articles about them.

Catalog note: results are structured directory facts, not editorial pieces — for articles, AV, interviews, or research summaries about a project, use the lumenloop semantic and directory ops.

entries.description: Vector search over the Stellar research corpus. Semantic ($vectorSearch over Voyage embeddings) search across the Stellar knowledge corpus — SDF blog, SCF Handbook, SEPs/standards, dev docs, papers, SCF proposals, security audits, incident reports, and Lumenloop/EC research. Returns the top matching text chunks with source attribution, section, URL, and a confidence score (relevance + freshness + authority); audit chunks add auditor/protocol/severity. Filterable by source; falls back to BM25-lite keyword search if vectors are unavailable. Use when: 'how does X work', 'is X possible / has X been discussed on Stellar', 'what does the SEP/spec/audit say about X', or you need primary-source citations for a thesis or design question. Not for: what products exist or their funding/status → use searchProjects; GitHub source code ranked by quality → use searchRepos. Use for ecosystem KNOWLEDGE & explainer questions — 'what is / how does / who / why / is it true that…'. Topics: security & risk (bug bounty, vulnerability disclosure, security audit, exploit, hack, incident, post-mortem, oracle manipulation, YieldBlox, Reflector); compliance & regulation (Travel Rule, FATF, KYC, AML, BSA, sanctions, humanitarian aid); funding & governance (SCF v7.0, award tiers, grant programs, Neural Quorum Governance, community voting, quorum); the Stellar Development Foundation (SDF) — mission, legal structure, org; protocol history (Stellar Consensus Protocol, SCP, whitepaper, authors); ecosystem programs (ambassador program, regional chapters, India, bootcamps); and fact-checking or verifying a claim about Stellar. More topics this corpus answers: SCF program mechanics — how to apply (step-by-step application process), Build Award, Instawards, Liquidity Award (amounts), Public Goods Award, eligibility, review timeline (prescreen, panel review, community vote, KYC), submission deadlines; Soroban security incidents — reentrancy, soroban-sdk security advisories/CVEs, denial-of-service (instance-storage growth, authorization recursion); ecosystem history — Soroban mainnet launch (Protocol 20), XLM initial supply creation and distribution, humanitarian aid disbursements (UNHCR Ukraine), SDF Enterprise Fund investments, SDF mandate/spending priorities; making an issued asset tradable/visible on exchanges, wallets, explorers, and aggregators; contract source verification (release.yml, Stellar Lab vs stellar.expert).

Covers Groth16 verification in smart contracts via BLS12-381 host functions (CAP-0059, available), the BN254 + Poseidon proposals (CAP-0074/0075, status-sensitive), and concrete toolchain walkthroughs for Circom (on-chain verifiable today), Noir, and RISC Zero (attestation pattern until BN254 lands). Fetch all indexed sections (heading anchors + content) of a single Stellar developer docs page identified by its URL path, e.g. /docs/build/smart-contracts/getting-started/storing-data or /docs/learn/fundamentals/stellar-consensus-protocol. Use after a search hit to read a whole page section by section instead of a single snippet — the docs equivalent of opening the page. Returns every section in on-page order with anchor, heading breadcrumb and full content text.

includeContent.description: Include each section's full content text (default true — this op exists to read pages).

path.description: URL path of the page on developers.stellar.org, no domain, no anchor — e.g. /docs/build/smart-contracts/getting-started/storing-data. Use url_without_anchor from a previous search hit.

provenance.note: authored spec-as-data operation (Lane D todo 796), live-verified against the Algolia index — not a fetched descriptor

clientFilter.why: URL is not a facet; the adapter queries with the derived title words (distinct disabled to get every section record) and keeps only records of the requested page, sorted by weight.position when retrieved.

algolia.derivedQuery: tokens of the path's last segment, hyphens split — e.g. 'storing data' from .../storing-data
algolia.fallback: if the derived query returns no records for the page, retry with the last two path segments as the query; zero matches after that = path not in the index.

entries.description:
Search Stellar docs about Soroban smart contract development in Rust: getting started (setup, hello world, storing data, deploy to testnet), 25 example contracts, contract storage (persistent vs temporary vs instance, TTL, state archival, restoring archived entries), authorization and custom accounts (__check_auth, contract accounts), cross-contract calls, events (emit and read back), errors and debugging, testing guides (unit tests, mutation, fuzzing, ledger snapshots), conventions (no Rust std, no floating point, wasm build), transaction simulation (simulateTransaction), fees and metering, deploying and invoking with the Stellar CLI, and contract-development fundamentals (environment, host functions, versioning across protocol releases). Covers /docs/build/smart-contracts, /docs/build/guides and /docs/learn/fundamentals/contract-development (~170 pages).

Returns: Array of contract-development hits: { url, url_without_anchor, anchor, breadcrumb, type, snippet }.

provenance.note: authored spec-as-data operation (Lane D todo 796), live-verified against the Algolia index — not a fetched descriptor

constraints.advancedSyntax: quoted "exact phrases" and -exclusions work directly in `query`; queryType prefixLast matches the final word as a prefix.
constraints.apiReferencePagesNotIndexed: Auto-generated per-method RPC reference pages (e.g. /docs/data/apis/rpc/api-reference/methods/getLedgerEntries) and per-endpoint Horizon reference pages are NOT in the index. Only hand-written structure/errors/admin-guide pages are. Method-specific how-tos live under /docs/build/guides/rpc and /docs/build/guides/transactions.
constraints.distinct: distinct:true on url by default — hits are deduped per URL+anchor; nbHits counts distinct groups. distinct:0 reveals raw per-section records.
constraints.hierarchyLvl0NotFacetable: facetFilters on hierarchy.lvl0 silently return nbHits: 0 (no error). Category restriction must happen adapter-side by url_without_anchor prefix (see per-op `clientFilter`).

entries.description:
Search Stellar docs about building applications, wallets and dapps: the Build a Wallet with the Wallet SDK tutorial series (including anchor SEP-24 deposit/withdrawal integration), Build a Payment App with the JS SDK tutorial, the passkey-powered guestbook dapp (smart wallets, WebAuthn passkeys, secp256r1 signature verification), dapp frontend guide and dapp development guides (initialization, state archival handling in dapps, working with deployed contracts), Freighter browser-extension wallet integration guides, the Swift payment app, payment tracking with the ingest SDK, ZK (zero-knowledge) apps, privacy considerations and application design considerations. Covers /docs/build/apps, /docs/build/guides/dapps and /docs/build/guides/freighter (~57 pages).

Returns: Array of app/wallet/dapp hits: { url, url_without_anchor, anchor, breadcrumb, type, snippet }.
